column2: DOT | UPN and logon name differ (DOT-4142)
column6: This test case ensures that radius can authenticate a host using dns suffixes.
column16: TC used with different configurations: (Kerberos with Global Catalog TLS (port 3269), (Kerberos standard LDAP (port 389), Kerberos with Global Catalog (port 3268), Kerberos with Global Catalog TLS (port 3269)
column20:
Prerequisites:


Perform QA-8827 (Configuration)
Expected Result:
Test case successfully completed.
Windows endpoint joined to a domain set up for authentication.
Expected Result:
Successfully have a windows endpoint joined to a domain.
Configure UPN/Username: 


On a domain controller, go to start > administrative tools > active directory users and computers
Click Users
Find the user that will be used for the test
Double click the user
Go to the account tab
Change the user logon name so that it is different from pre-windows 2000 logon name
Apply

Note:  You can use Rob Hughes account (if not removed)
![](index.php?/attachments/get/37726)


Expected Result:
Changes saved successfully
On the CLI of the EM run the following command: fstool oneach fstool dot1x set_property config.upn.doesntmatch.sam true
Expected Result:
Command was accepted
restart the dot1x plugin: fstool oneach fstool dot1x restart
Expected Result:
dot1x successfully restarted.
Double click the interface used for testing
Expected Result:
Connection properties are displayed
Click the authentication tab
Click on Additional Settings.
Expected Result:
Option deselected
Authentication : 


Save/Replace credentials for User authenication using the username format txqalab\username and save.

![](index.php?/attachments/get/37727)

Make sure endpoint connects and authenticates.

Expected Result:
Verify that you can authenticate successfully.
Authentication : 


Save/Replace credentials for User authenication using the username format username@domain and save.

![](index.php?/attachments/get/37728)

Make sure endpoint connects and authenticates.

Expected Result:
Verify that you can authenticate successfully.
* In the CounterACT console select the host that you just authenticated. 
* Under the profile section select Authentication
Expected Result:
CounterACT successfully showing all the radius properties of the endpoint.

column2: DOT | Authenticating Using LDAP Group
column6: This test case ensures that having the pre-admission condition of verifying the LDAP group works
column9: Have a switch or wireless device setup for radius authentication
column16: TC used with different configurations: (Kerberos with Global Catalog TLS (port 3269), (Kerberos standard LDAP (port 389), Kerberos with Global Catalog (port 3268), Kerberos with Global Catalog TLS (port 3269)
column20:
Configure an Authentication source in CA that can handle LDAP requests. The Plano active directory 10.100.127.1 can handle so use this one if possible
Expected Result:
Authentication source configured
In CA go to Options -> Radius -> Pre-admission Authorization and add a rule with condition "LDAP-Group". This will look for the same groups as in the "Member of Group", so if using your adm account you can use "Domain Users", "Domain Admins", or "Domain*". Move the rule to be the 1st rule in the priority order
Expected Result:
Rule created successfully
Log into the endpoint under test using a user in the Active Directory. For example, you can use your "first initials"+"last name"+"-adm" account

![](index.php?/attachments/get/37681)

Expected Result:
User logged in with active directory account
Connect endpoint to device and have it send an access-request to CA
Expected Result:
User should be accepted and endpoint should be connected successfully
In host profile check Authentication 802.1x Authorization Source to verify the pre-admission rule # created earlier as rule 1 is chosen 
Expected Result:
Ex:

802.1x Authorization Source: Pre-Admission rule 1 
Disconnect endpoint.
Re-Authenticate endpoint using a Username that starts with **N, R, or T**
example: RThangaraj-adm

Expected Result:
User Authenticated and endpoint connected.

802.1x Authorization Source: **Pre-Admission rule 1**

column9:
###CounterAct User Directory configured
###CounterAct switch plugin configured for 802.1x authentication
###Lab switch configured for 802.1x authentication
###CounterAct Radius Configured
- The default Pre-Admission Auth Rule is set to Deny. ﻿![](index.php?/attachments/get/41485)

column16: TC used with different configurations: (Kerberos with Global Catalog TLS (port 3269), (Kerberos standard LDAP (port 389), Kerberos with Global Catalog (port 3268), Kerberos with Global Catalog TLS (port 3269)
column18:
From the CounterAct GUI go to **Options -> Radius** and select the **Pre-Admission Authorization** tab.

Press **Add**.
Press **Add** to create a Criterion Name.
From the Attributes select **EAP-Type**
For the value select **PEAP**
Press **OK** and **OK**
**Apply** the configuration.

Verify Configuration.
---
Configuration saved with no errors reported
Radius plugin restarts.

The Rule is added with a priority value of 1.
![](index.php?/attachments/get/12130)

---
Untested
Disconnect/Connect the Host computer to the network.

Verify the Host received an IP address. (CMD prompt - ipconfig)
---
The NIC adaptor contains a IP address from the VLAN configured on the switch Interface.
---
Untested
From the CounterAct GUI on the **Home** tab select the Host from the **ALL Host** table.

From the **Profile** section click on the Header **Authentication**

Verify Authentication details.


---
802.1x Authorization Source: **Pre-Admission rule 1**
802.1x RADIUS Authentication State: **RADIUS-Accepted**
802.1x Authenticated Entity: **User** 
802.1x Authentication type: **PEAP**
802.1x Authenticating Appliance: <Received 802.1x request>
802.1x Last Authentication Time: <Correct Date/Time>
802.1x NAS IP Address: <Network device connected to Host>

Example:
![](index.php?/attachments/get/36039)



INFO:
The 802.1x Authenticating Appliance value will be the IP address configured in Network device as the Radius Authenticating server. 
---
Untested
On the Host change the Authenticating user name to a value that does not exist on the Active Directory.
Example:
txqalab\joenotfound
---
Verify from the Profile section, click on the Header Authentication and view **802.1x RADIUS Authentication State:** contains RADIUS-Rejected

Example:
![](index.php?/attachments/get/36340)

Verify the Host has no IP address on that NIC.

column6:
##Checks that a Endpoint can authenticate to more than one Domain

###If CounterAct version 9.1.2 (Red Hat 9.x) or later the following path should exist with the Dot1x version shown in Preconditions.
/usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/var/locks

- Path will contain the configured LDAP servers in Radius.
    - Enter: find .

    - Example:
    - ./.txqalab2
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_cache.tdb
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_privileged
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_privileged/pipe
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_cache.tdb.bak
    - ./.txqalab
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_cache.tdb
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_privileged
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_privileged/pipe
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_cache.tdb.bak

###if CounterAct version less than 9.1.2 the **Locks** directory will not exist.
column9:
###Dot1x version 4.8.1-48010204 or higher required if CounterAct version 9.1.2 (Red Hat 9.x) or higher

###CounterAct User Directory configured
- **AD serverA**: **IP** - 10.100.127.1, **Server NAME** - TXQALAB-DC1, **Domain** - txqalab.forescout.local
    - User - administrator, PW - aristo
- **AD serverB**: **IP** - 10.100.127.2, **Server NAME** - TXQALAB2-DC1, **Domain** - txqalab2.txqalab.forescout.local
    - User - admin, PW - aristo
- UD test should pass

###CounterAct switch and/or wireless plugin configured for 802.1x authentication
###Lab switch and/or wireless controller configured for 802.1x authentication (PEAP)
###CounterAct Radius Configured
- Add the two Sources configured in the User Directory
    - Radius Test should **Pass** for AD ServerA and B.
###Endpoint-A configured to perform 802.1x authentication "PEAP"
- User Account on the txqalab.forescout.local Domain.
    - Ex: txqalab\<your name>-adm
###Endpoint-B configured to perform 802.1x authentication "PEAP"
- User Account located on txqalab2.txqalab.forescout.local Domain.
    - User - **txqalab2\e2euser**, PW - aristo

###Note: One Endpoint can be used by changing the Username.

column2: DOT | Verify authenticating a Endpoint to multiple Domains
column6:
##Checks that a Endpoint can authenticate to more than one Domain

###If CounterAct version 9.1.2 (Red Hat 9.x) or later the following path should exist with the Dot1x version shown in Preconditions.
/usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/var/locks

- Path will contain the configured LDAP servers in Radius.
    - Enter: find .

    - Example:
    - ./.txqalab2
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_cache.tdb
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_privileged
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_privileged/pipe
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_cache.tdb.bak
    - ./.txqalab
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_cache.tdb
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_privileged
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_privileged/pipe
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_cache.tdb.bak

###if CounterAct version less than 9.1.2 the **Locks** directory will not exist.
column9:
###Dot1x version 4.8.1-48010204 or higher required if CounterAct version 9.1.2 (Red Hat 9.x) or higher

###CounterAct User Directory configured
- **AD serverA**: **IP** - 10.100.127.1, **Server NAME** - TXQALAB-DC1, **Domain** - txqalab.forescout.local
    - User - administrator, PW - aristo
- **AD serverB**: **IP** - 10.100.127.2, **Server NAME** - TXQALAB2-DC1, **Domain** - txqalab2.txqalab.forescout.local
    - User - admin, PW - aristo
- UD test should pass

###CounterAct switch and/or wireless plugin configured for 802.1x authentication
###Lab switch and/or wireless controller configured for 802.1x authentication (PEAP)
###CounterAct Radius Configured
- Add the two Sources configured in the User Directory
    - Radius Test should **Pass** for AD ServerA and B.
###Endpoint-A configured to perform 802.1x authentication "PEAP"
- User Account on the txqalab.forescout.local Domain.
    - Ex: txqalab\<your name>-adm
###Endpoint-B configured to perform 802.1x authentication "PEAP"
- User Account located on txqalab2.txqalab.forescout.local Domain.
    - User - **txqalab2\e2euser**, PW - aristo

###Note: One Endpoint can be used by changing the Username.

column2: DOT | Verify authenticating a Endpoint to multiple Domains
column6:
##Checks that a Endpoint can authenticate to more than one Domain

###If CounterAct version 9.1.2 (Red Hat 9.x) or later the following path should exist with the Dot1x version shown in Preconditions.
/usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/var/locks

- Path will contain the configured LDAP servers in Radius.
    - Enter: find .

    - Example:
    - ./.txqalab2
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_cache.tdb
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_privileged
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_privileged/pipe
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_cache.tdb.bak
    - ./.txqalab
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_cache.tdb
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_privileged
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_privileged/pipe
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_cache.tdb.bak

###if CounterAct version less than 9.1.2 the **Locks** directory will not exist.
column9:
###Dot1x version 4.8.1-48010204 or higher required if CounterAct version 9.1.2 (Red Hat 9.x) or higher

###CounterAct User Directory configured
- **AD serverA**: **IP** - 10.100.127.1, **Server NAME** - TXQALAB-DC1, **Domain** - txqalab.forescout.local
    - User - administrator, PW - aristo
- **AD serverB**: **IP** - 10.100.127.2, **Server NAME** - TXQALAB2-DC1, **Domain** - txqalab2.txqalab.forescout.local
    - User - admin, PW - aristo
- UD test should pass

###CounterAct switch and/or wireless plugin configured for 802.1x authentication
###Lab switch and/or wireless controller configured for 802.1x authentication (PEAP)
###CounterAct Radius Configured
- Add the two Sources configured in the User Directory
    - Radius Test should **Pass** for AD ServerA and B.
###Endpoint-A configured to perform 802.1x authentication "PEAP"
- User Account on the txqalab.forescout.local Domain.
    - Ex: txqalab\<your name>-adm
###Endpoint-B configured to perform 802.1x authentication "PEAP"
- User Account located on txqalab2.txqalab.forescout.local Domain.
    - User - **txqalab2\e2euser**, PW - aristo

###Note: One Endpoint can be used by changing the Username.

Click Users
Find the user that will be used for the test

Apply

restart the dot1x plugin: fstool oneach fstool dot1x restart

dot1x successfully restarted.
Double click the interface used for testing
Expected Result:
Connection properties are displayed
Click the authentication tab
Click on Additional Settings.

Option deselected

Authentication source configured

This will look for the same groups as in the "Member of Group", so if using your adm account you can use "Domain Users", "Domain Admins", or "Domain*". Move the rule to be the 1st rule in the priority order

802.1x Authorization Source: Pre-Admission rule 1

Re-Authenticate endpoint using a Username that starts with **N, R, or T**
example: RThangaraj-adm

User Authenticated and endpoint connected.

column2: DOT | Verify authenticating a Endpoint to multiple Domains
column6:
##Checks that a Endpoint can authenticate to more than one Domain

###If CounterAct version 9.1.2 (Red Hat 9.x) or later the following path should exist with the Dot1x version shown in Preconditions.
/usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/var/locks

- Path will contain the configured LDAP servers in Radius.
    - Enter: find .

    - Example:
    - ./.txqalab2
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_cache.tdb
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_privileged
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_privileged/pipe
    - ./.txqalab2/winbindd_cache.tdb.bak
    - ./.txqalab
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_cache.tdb
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_privileged
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_privileged/pipe
    - ./.txqalab/winbindd_cache.tdb.bak

###if CounterAct version less than 9.1.2 the **Locks** directory will not exist.
column9:
###Dot1x version 4.8.1-48010204 or higher required if CounterAct version 9.1.2 (Red Hat 9.x) or higher

###CounterAct User Directory configured
- **AD serverA**: **IP** - 10.100.127.1, **Server NAME** - TXQALAB-DC1, **Domain** - txqalab.forescout.local
    - User - administrator, PW - aristo
- **AD serverB**: **IP** - 10.100.127.2, **Server NAME** - TXQALAB2-DC1, **Domain** - txqalab2.txqalab.forescout.local
    - User - admin, PW - aristo
- UD test should pass

###CounterAct switch and/or wireless plugin configured for 802.1x authentication
###Lab switch and/or wireless controller configured for 802.1x authentication (PEAP)
###CounterAct Radius Configured
- Add the two Sources configured in the User Directory
    - Radius Test should **Pass** for AD ServerA and B.
###Endpoint-A configured to perform 802.1x authentication "PEAP"
- User Account on the txqalab.forescout.local Domain.
    - Ex: txqalab\<your name>-adm
###Endpoint-B configured to perform 802.1x authentication "PEAP"
- User Account located on txqalab2.txqalab.forescout.local Domain.
    - User - **txqalab2\e2euser**, PW - aristo

###Note: One Endpoint can be used by changing the Username.

Ex: A case where the plugin crash during start/restart but still comes up running and operating as expected.

It is preferred that the health check be run multiple times during the test cycle.

If all works well fs_clients.conf should not be found in /usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/etc/raddb

    - On CounterAct within Trusted Certificates: Import **Dot1x-CA.pem** - Get cert at [Cert Download](https://testrail/index.php?/cases/view/148237)

column2: DOT | Radius Dictionaries Extracted On Fresh Console Install
column6:
This test case is to help ensure that when you do a fresh installation of the CounterACT console, the Radius Dictionary files, which are gziped, are un-gziped and extracted into the correct directory.  When there is an issue, you will see the Radius Dictionary files still gziped on the machine where you installed the console under:  
C:\<CounterACT Install Directory>\GuiManager\current\etc\gui\files\radius_dictionaries  
(ex: C:\Users\gchan\Forescout Console 8.4.0\GuiManager\current\etc\gui\files\radius_dictionaries)

When this happens, you might see this under Options->Radius and PreAdmission Authorization
![](index.php?/attachments/get/38108)

Because the Radius dictionary files were not extracted, you will see a "FileNotFoundException" in the Trace_gui_xxx.txt file on the console machine.  
Location of file: C:\<CounterACT Install Directory>\GuiManager\current\log
column20:
From a Windows machine, perform a fresh installation of the CounterACT console.  

Note:  If you already have a console installed on the machine, just perform an installation in a "new" install directory.
Expected Result:
Verify that the installation completes successfully.
Check to see that the Radius dictionary file is un-gziped.
Go to C:\<CounterACT Install Directory>\GuiManager\current\etc\gui\files\radius_dictionaries
Expected Result:
Verify that there files extracted in the directory.
ex:
![](index.php?/attachments/get/38111)

Go to Options->Radius and PreAdmission Authorization
Expected Result:
Verify that the panels display correctly.
Change the "Minimum TLS version: and then hit Apply.
Expected Result:
Verify that you are able to change the version and save.
column23: Verify that the installation completes successfully.

##Radius, User Directory, switch/Wireless device already configured in CounterAct.
- From CounterAct add a Radius Rule **EAP Type** = **TLS**.

- From CounterAct import **Dot1x-server.pfx** within System Certificates. **[See section Import System Certificates](https://testrail/index.php?/cases/view/72121)**

- Note the uptime for each plugin

    - Subordinate processes:

    - **winbindd (pid 90216) of txqalab is running for 28:47.**
    - **redis-server (pid 84705) is running for 30:06.**

column2: DOT | Verify setting the Fragment size to 1024 (Default)
column6:
The MTU is being set to 994 and was causing a issue for a switch set with a MTU of 0

- MTU value - 30 = Framed-MTU

The three attached files (test_fragment-size_eq_xxxx) contain the output from the EAPOL Tool when it successfully authenticates and showing the MTU value sent.

Example output:
Received RADIUS message
RADIUS message: code=2 (Access-Accept)
 Attribute 12 (Framed-MTU) length=6
      Value: 970

You can test the packet size that can be sent between two network devices by using **Ping**

- From CounterAct: **ping -M do -s <packet size> <destination IP>**

**Ex:** ping -M do -s 1472 10.16.128.12

- -M do - Don't Fragment
- -s packet size in bytes ( 28 bytes added to the value entered do to ICMP header + data, IP header, and Frame size.)

1472 becomes 1500

Note: The MTU on counterAct can be checked by entering **ifconfig**

- eth0: flags=4163<UP,BROADCAST,RUNNING,MULTICAST>  mtu 1500
column8:
1. Change the **Fragment size** back  to the default 1024
2. **Apply Configuration**
column9:
###CountrAct: Switch, User Directory and Radius plugin configured.
- Certificates for Radius imported
###Switch or Wireless: Configured for 802.1x
###Endpoint: Configured for 802.1 and certificates imported
- Requires PEAP, EAP-TLS, and PEAP-EAP-TLS authentication.
column20:
##From CounterAct GUI 
1. Go to **Options** -> **Radius** > **Radius Setting**
2. Set the Fragment size to **1024** if not at this value.
- **This should be the default value if never changed.**

Note: 1024 is what the default value should be if never changed.
Expected Result:
Configuration saved
##From CounterAct GUI 
1. Go to **Options** -> **Radius** > **Pre-Admission Authorization**
2. Add a Rule with a Condition that contains the Criterion name **EAP-Type** with a value of **PEAP**
3. Press **OK** and **OK**.
4. Add a Rule with a Condition that contains the Criterion name **EAP-Type** with a value of **EAP-TLS**
5. Press **OK** and **OK**
6. **Apply Configuration**
Expected Result:
Configuration applied
Force the Endpoint to authenticate via **PEAP-EAP-TLS**
Expected Result:
Verify the GUI shows it authenticated.

- Search from the bottom of the file up for **Sent Access-Accept**

column2: DOT | CoA with customizable AVP : Action: Force Re-Authorization
column6:
CoA with cutomizable AVP : Action: Force Re-Authorization

====

From the help file:  Cancelling the RADIUS Authorize Action :Cancelling the RADIUS Authorize action removes the authorization applied by the action and allows authorization to be applied as provided from the hierarchy of Forescout authorization sources.
#Cancellation of the authorization imposed on an endpoint only takes effect at the next authentication of the targeted endpoint.
Action cancellation occurs:
•
	
Following policy evaluation. For endpoints that no longer match a policy sub-rule and the action is defined for that sub-rule.
•
	
When the Forescout user manually cancels it.
•
	
When the settings of this action are changed and the action is re-applied on matching endpoints.

column9:
#CounterAct System Setup
- EM managing two Appliances (APP1 and APP2)
- Radius and UD configured
- Switch or WLC plugin on **APP2** is managing the Network Device and configured for 802.1x [see Description above for where below config is set]
    - **CoA Port** is set to **3799** or Cisco default 1700.
    - **CoA Identification Attributes** are set to **Custom** and all options supported by the switch/WLC are selected.
        - **Note**: Not all options supported and a NAK is returned if the option is not supported.
    - Segment or Segments created and assigned to APP2 that contain the IP address of the host for each VLAN that can be assigned.

#Network Device and Host Setup
- **APP1** IP address is configured in the switch or WLC as the Radius Server
- Switch/WLC configured to perform 802.1x and receive CoA Request from **APP1**
- Switch/WLC contains both VLANS to be used during a CoA action.
- Host configured to perform 802.1x authentication. (ALL types supported)

###Summary:
**APP1** Handling 802.1x communication to the Network Device
**APP2** Managing the Host (Based on Segment assignment) and the Network Device (Based on Plugin configuration) from the CounterAct GUI.

#Host State
- Host authenticated via Radius and is shown on the **Home** tab within the **ALL Host** table

Press **Add** to create a Criterion Name.

For the value select **PEAP**

Configuration saved with no errors reported
Radius plugin restarts.

column2: DOT | Enable OCSP and Connecting with a "Good" Client Certificate
column6: This test case is testing OCSP with a client certificate that is "good" and will allow for authentication.
column9:
See [C146384] for details on how to create the certificates and supporting files to run this test.  Or just use the files attached to this test cases and place them in the appropriate areas.

For this test, you will need to have a web server setup.  (If none available, Google how to install NGINX on a Linux box and you can easily create one).  There is a web server running NGINX that can be used: 10.16.190.5 (fsadmin/aristo). The default document directory is /usr/share/nginx/html

If using the attached zip file, unzip the file and place in the appropriate places.  See [C146384] on how to import certain files to different places:
**CounterACTFiles:**
> CRL_CA_Test1.pem  (import to Trusted Certificates on CounterACT)
> server.pfx  (import to System Certificates on CounterACT)

**WebServerFiles:**
> ca.crt
> CRL_CA_Test1.pem
> crl-ca.crl
> ocsp.conf
> ocsp.crt
> ocsp.db
> ocsp.key

- Place these files in the directory that your web server reads from.  (ex. NGINX is defaulted at /usr/share/nginx/html)

![](index.php?/attachments/get/75647)

**WindowsFiles:**
> client1.pfx
> client2.pfx

- Only place one of these files at a time.  'client1.pfx' is used if you want to test a 'good' certificate.  'client2.pfx' is used if you want to test a 'revoked' certificate.
column20:
Once the files/certificates are placed in the appropriate places, start the OCSP Responder on the webserver by bringing up a ssh session, going to where you have placed your files and run the following:
**cd /usr/share/nginx/html**
**openssl ocsp -rsigner ocsp.crt -rkey ocsp.key -CA ca.crt -index ocsp.db -port 8080**
(syntax of this command could change depending on what version of openssl you have)
Expected Result:
You should see:  
Waiting for OCSP client connections...
From CounterACT, go to Options->Radius->Radius Settings
Click on Enable OCSP using FreeRadius built-in support for OCSP Responder and Override Certificate OCSP URL.
For OCSP Responder URL, use http://web server ip:8080
![](index.php?/attachments/get/41775)


On your Windows host, disconnect and connect the interface.
Expected Result:

column2: DOT | OCSP Other CA Support - with a "Good" Client Certificate
column6: This test case will be testing a "good" client certificate that is signed by a CA that is different than the CA certificate for the OCSP Responder.
column9:
For testing, you will need a web server.  You can setup one and install NGINX on it.  

You must have all the certificates/files generated already for the Windows client, CounterACT, and web server.  See [C148197] on how to setup testing with OCSP where the OCSP Responder certificate is signed by "other" CA.  I have also attached a zipfile that has all the certificates/files.  You can just use these files and place them in the appropriate places.

**CounterACTFiles:**
> ca1.pem  (import to Trusted Certificates on CounterACT)
> server.pfx  (import to System Certificates on CounterACT)
> ocsp2.crt (OCSP Responder for the "other" CA - placed under /usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/var)

**WebServerFiles:**
>ca1.crt  (this is the CA cert for the CA that validates the client certificates--not the "other" CA)
>ocsp2.conf  
>ocsp2.crt (this is the OCSP Responder that is signed by the "other" CA) 
>ocsp2.db (certificate index file)
>ocsp2.key (OCSP Responder Private Key) 

Place these files in the directory that your web server reads from.  (ex. NGINX is defaulted at /usr/share/nginx/html)
![](index.php?/attachments/get/75644)


**WindowsFiles:**
> client1.pfx - good certificate
> client2.pfx - revoked certificate
> client3.pfx - expired certificate

Only place one of these files at a time.  'client1.pfx' is used if you want to test a 'good' certificate.  'client2.pfx' is used if you want to test a 'revoked' certificate.  'client3.pfx' is used if you want to test an expired certificate.
column20:

**openssl ocsp -rsigner ocsp2.crt -rkey ocsp2.key -CA ca1.crt -index ocsp2.db -port 8080**
(syntax of this command could change depending on what version of openssl you have)

- Click on Enable OCSP using OpenSSL shell support for OCSP Responder.

DOT | Verify Authentication using a WLC set for Generic Radius | Wireless
column6:
This verifies when the wireless plugin is configured for Generic WLC the Radius server can authenticate Endpoints.
Note: This is related to DOT-5139 but did not duplicate invalid secret using the configuration in this test case. This test case did duplicate the secret not being removed, missing "/32", and invalid/duplicate headers added to the "fs_clients_enc.conf".

**To truly test this it needs to be done on the Cost system that has many appliances. It is still unclear if Over-Lapping is required but Cost has this.**

In order to check the encrypted value update the enc.pl file (un-comment two lines and comment out another) located at "/usr/local/forescout/lib/fstool/commands" as shown
![](index.php?/attachments/get/59075)
Note: no restart required

- On a 9.1.2 system need to add the two lines below if not shown as above:

  my $clear = fs_decrypt($opt_d);
  print defined $clear ? "$clear\n" : "Not an encrypted string\n";
column9:
###User Directory and Radius plugin configured
###Endpoint configured for 802.1x.
###Wireless controller WLAN configured for 802.1x.
column20:
A. On CounterAct **Options -> Wireless** Configure the wireless plugin with the following:
- ![](index.php?/attachments/get/59031)

- Enter the IP address of the WLC and add a CIDR value of **32**.
    - Ex: ![](index.php?/attachments/get/59032)
- Configure SNMP
- Configure a **invalid** Radius Secret.
Apply

B. On CounterAct **Options -> Wireless** add two more entries as above using unused IP address within your vlan. (not real WLC)
Apply
Expected Result:
A. Configuration applied.
Ex:
![](index.php?/attachments/get/59033)

B. Configuration applied
Attempted to authenticate the Endpoint
Expected Result:
Verify authentication fails in the log Due to **invalid Message-Authenticator!  (Shared secret is incorrect)**
On CounterAct **Options -> Wireless** Update the Radius secret to the same value configured in the WLC.

Update the secret to a different value on the two WLC that are not real.
Apply


Expected Result:
Configuration Saved

The fs_clients_enc.conf file contains a new secret value.
/usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/etc/raddb/fs_clients_enc.conf

Verify the header (client1, client2, and so on) is not duplicated in /usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/etc/raddb/fs_clients_enc.conf
Attempted to authenticate the Endpoint
Expected Result:
Verify the Endpoint Authenticates:
Gui show **RADIUS-Accepted** for the correct Authentication Time.
Endpoint contains a IP address within the configured VLAN.
See **Description** for information on how to allow decrypt of secret.


A. View and decrypt the stored secret in "fs_clients_enc.conf" for the WLC on each appliance located at: /usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/etc/raddb/fs_clients_enc.conf

Enter: fstool enc -d <encrypted string>


B. View and decrypt the stored secret in the DB for the WLC on each appliance.
To view the DB enter: 
psql -c "select id, time, field_name, field_value from devinfo where field_name like '%_dot1x_radius_secret'"

To decrypt secret enter: fstool enc -d <encrypted string>
Expected Result:
A. File should contain the IP mask and the encrypted secret. There should not be a duplicate header. (client1, client2, and so on not duplicated)
Ex:
![](index.php?/attachments/get/60581)

The decrypted secret matches the value configured in the wireless plugin

B. The value in the DB should have IP mask and the encrypted secret.
Ex:
![](index.php?/attachments/get/60582)

The decrypted secret matches the value configured in the wireless plugin

Note: The encrypted string will be different on each CounterAct device and between the file and DB on the same counterAct device.

column19:
#1.
From the Windows host disable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
From CounterAct **Options -> Radius > Pre-Admission Authentication**.
- Select **ADD** to create a new Rule
- For the **Condition** section press **ADD**
    - From the **Attributes** list select **Certificate-Microsoft-Certificate-Authority**
    - For the value select **.2** and **.4**, **.6**, **.8**, **.14**, **.16**, **.22**, and **.32**.
    - Press **OK** and **OK**
- Press **Apply**
###See section Expected Result number 1
___

#2.
From the host enable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
###See section Expected Result number 2
___

#3.
From the Windows host disable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
From CounterAct update the value for the Condition by deselecting **.2** only.
Apply the configuration
From the host enable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
###See section Expected Result number 3
___

#4.
From the Windows host disable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
From CounterAct update the value for the Condition by unselecting all but **.14** and **.22**.
Apply the configuration
From the host enable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
###See section Expected Result number 4
___

#5.
From the Windows host disable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
From CounterAct update the value for the Condition by unselecting all but **.14** and **.32**.
Apply the configuration
From the host Delete the **Dot1xMSCA-CLT-B** cert from the **Personal > Certificate** folder seen within the **MMC** application.
From the Host import certificate **Dot1xMSCA-CLT-C.pfx** to the **Personal > Certificate** folder. (Contains: .2 and .32)

- After import delete the CA certificate named **Dot1x-CA** within the **Personal > Certificate** folder. (This is the **Issue To** name)

From the host enable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
###See section Expected Result number 5
___

#6.
From the Windows host disable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
From CounterAct update the value for the Condition by selecting **All** options.
Apply the configuration
From the host Delete the **Dot1xMSCA-CLT-C** cert from the Personal > Certificate folder seen within the MMC application.
From the Host import certificate **Dot1xMSCA-CLT-D.pfx** to the **Personal > Certificate folder**. (Cert contains no MSCA values)

- After import delete the CA certificate named **Dot1x-CA** within the Personal > Certificate folder. (This is the "Issue To" name)

From the host enable the Network Connection connected to the device configured for 802.1x authentication.
###See section Expected Result number 6

column2: DOT | Pre-Admission rule - MAC Found in MAR mismatch
column6:
This test case is for confirming the fix made for MAC queries in MAR in pre-admission rules for RADIUS. 

When using a pre-admission rule using condition "MAC Found in MAR" in 4.7.5-47050090, the condition does not match as intended. This is due to a missing regex update in a certain location (extra/plugin/dot1x/fr_config/policy.d/ntlm_auth) when this regex was updated in another location (extra/plugin/dot1x/fr_config/policy.d/fs_policy) for a separate fix - https://jira.fsd.forescout.com/browse/DOT-4924

For this test, we should be able to correctly match with a MAC in the MAR. This test will be conducted  by allowing/denying access based on a match.

Get the "NAS-Identifier from the the connected vendor by running:  

fstool dot1x debug 4
cd /usr/local/forescout/log/plugin/dot1x
tail -f radiusd.log | grep -i 'Access-Accept\|rule_\|tunnel\| NAS-Identifier'

Authenticate the wireless user and note the NAS-Identifier provided.

column2: DOT | "Authentication Source" Prevents Radiusd From Starting
column6:
After upgrading to Radius Plugin 4.7.6, if you remove the Ad server listed as an Authentication Source in the GUI, the configuration file ldap_[DOMAIN NAME] does not get removed from /usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/etc/raddb/mods-enabled on the appliance and Radiusd wouldn't start because it keep trying to bind invalid ldap_[DOMAIN NAME] server.

The workaround this issue was the following:
> Temporary solution:
> Stop the Radius plugin
> Go to the following directories
> /usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/etc/raddb/ldap/
> /usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/etc/raddb/mods-enabled/
> Remove the invalid authorization source ldap_[source name]

This test case is to test the fix for the above issue and not have to do the workaround.

column2: DOT | Verify Radiusd Stays Up After Losing Connection to AD and DOT1X Restart
column6: This test case is to test that Radiusd states up and running after it loses connectivity to the Active Directory Server and after a dot1x restart.
column9:
Have Radius configured with a valid Authentication Source provisioned.  Make sure that radiusd and winbind are running and not dead (fstool dot1x status).
You will also need to have a switch and two endpoint connected and authenticated via PEAP and MAB with radius.
column16: Test the ability to gracefully handle network outages, invalid configuration ..
column20:
From the managing Appliance, bring up a ssh session to monitor the radius status

> watch fstool dot1x status

Authenticate Hosts via PEAP and MAB
Expected Result:
Verify that both radiusd and winbind are up and running.

Verify Host can authenticate
From another ssh window to the Appliance, we will need to block all traffic to the Active Directory using firewall rules:

> fstool fw addhook <name> "iptables -I OUTPUT 1 -d <address to block> -j DROP"

Example:  
fstool fw addhook test1 "iptables -I OUTPUT 1 -d 10.100.127.1 -j DROP"
fstool fw addhook test2 "iptables -I OUTPUT 1 -d 10.100.127.3 -j DROP"
Disconnect the NIC for both endpoints and reconnect it.
Expected Result:
Verify that the endpoint performing PEAP Auth can not authenticate and the endpoint performing MAB is successful.  Also verify radiusd still running.

Note: Winbindd may or may not be shown, both are ok
Restart radius plugin.  You can either do a 'fstool dot1x restart' or make a simple change in the console for radius and apply and the radius plugin will restart.
Expected Result:
Verify after the restart, radiusd is running and winbind will show dead or not listed (Not running).
Disconnect the NIC for both endpoints and reconnect it.
Expected Result:
Verify that the endpoint performing PEAP Auth can not authenticate and the endpoint performing MAB is successful.
Remove the firewall rule that blocks traffic to the Active Directory server

> fstool fw delhook <name>

Example:
fstool fw delhook test1
fstool fw delhook test2


Expected Result:
Verify that radiusd and winbind are running.

Verify they remain running for 3 minutes minimum.

Note: Radiusd and Winbindd will restart to recover.
Disconnect your endpoints and then reconnect it.
Expected Result:
Verify that both endpoints (PEAP and MAB) authenticate via radius.

###Verify the host-A successfully authenticated: 
 
802.1x Authorization Source: Pre-Admission rule <x>

802.1x Last Authentication Time: <Correct Date/Time>
802.1x NAS IP Address: <Network device connected to Host>

###Block all traffic to the Active Directory using firewall rules:
From the Appliance handling Radius Authentication for the switch enter:

fstool fw addhook <name> "iptables -I OUTPUT 1 -d <address to block> -j DROP"

Example (AD = 10.100.127.1):
fstool fw addhook test1 "iptables -I OUTPUT 1 -d 10.100.127.1 -j DROP"
fstool fw addhook test2 "iptables -I OUTPUT 1 -d 10.100.127.3 -j DROP"

###From Host-A (PEAP)
1. Disable the NIC on the host
2. Enable the NIC on the host


Expected Result:
Verify from the GUI Host-A failed to authenticate
802.1x RADIUS Authentication State: **RADIUS-Rejected**
802.1x Authentication type: **PEAP**

###From CLI on the Appliance handling Radius Authentication
1. Enter: **fstool dot1x status**
2. Note the run time for **Radiusd**

###From the CounterAct GUI Options -> Wireless
1. Change the **Radius Secret** to a different value on the **802.1x** tab.
2. **Apply** config

###From CLI on the Appliance handling Radius Authentication
1. Enter: **fstool dot1x status**
Expected Result:
Verify **Radiusd** restarted and continues to run for at least a minute.
###From the CounterAct GUI Options -> Wireless
1. Change the **Radius Secret** back to the valid value on the **802.1x** tab.
2. **Apply** config

###From CLI on the Appliance handling Radius Authentication
1. Enter: **fstool dot1x status**

Expected Result:
Verify **Radiusd** restarted and continues to run for at least a minute.

###Remove the firewall rule that blocks traffic to the Active Directory server:
From the Appliance handling Radius Authentication for the switch enter:

fstool fw delhook <name>

fstool fw delhook test1
fstool fw delhook test2

column2: DOT | Verify Configuration is updated after adding a Device
column6:
This verifies when wireless and switch plugin is configured/updated the Radius configuration is updated.


Note: **To truly test this it needs to be done on the Cost system that has many appliances. It is still unclear if Over-Lapping is required but Cost has this.**

In order to check the encrypted value update the enc.pl file (un-comment two lines and comment out another) located at "/usr/local/forescout/lib/fstool/commands" as shown
![](index.php?/attachments/get/59075)
Note: no restart required

- On a 9.1.2 system need to add the two lines below if not shown as above:

  my $clear = fs_decrypt($opt_d);
  print defined $clear ? "$clear\n" : "Not an encrypted string\n";

##When adding the result for this TC note how many appliance where in the system.
column8: It highly recommended to export the devices added in switch and wireless. Other TC use this INFO.
column9:
###User Directory and Radius plugin configured
###A console is required to be installed on computer that is on the same Domain as the Wireless controller

##Note: The values entered for a switch and Wireless device is not required to be a real device.
column16: Validates CONF file, Radiusd restarts, Database, and Authentication
column20:
From the CounterAct GUI **Options -> Switch**

Add two devices using FQDN that are managed by the EM or Appliance.

- Enter a Radius secret. 

**Apply**

Check the Expected Results


Expected Result:
Verify Radiusd restarted by entering fstool dot1x status

Verify the devices where added to the fs_clients_enc.conf on the EM and appliances.

- /usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/etc/raddb/fs_clients_enc.conf
- Should contain FQDN

Verify the header (client1, client2, and so on) is not duplicated in fs_clients_enc.conf

Verify the DB contains the two devices added on the EM and Appliances.

- Enter: psql -c "select id, time, field_name, field_value from devinfo where field_name like '%_dot1x_radius_secret'"
- Should contain FQDN

Verify the Secret decrypts to the value entered for the devices on the EM and appliances.

- See Description for steps to allow decrypt of Secret.
- Enter: **fstool enc -d <hash value from DB>
- Enter: **fstool enc -d <hash value from fs_clients_enc.conf>



From the CounterAct GUI **Options -> Switch**

Add two Devices using IPv4 address that are managed by the EM or Appliance.

- Enter a Radius secret

**Apply**

Check the Expected Results

Verify Radiusd restarted by entering fstool dot1x status

- /usr/local/forescout/plugin/dot1x/fs_radius/etc/raddb/fs_clients_enc.conf
- Should contain IP Address

Verify the header (client1, client2, and so on) is not duplicated in fs_clients_enc.conf

Verify the DB contains the two devices added on the EM and Appliances.

- Enter: psql -c "select id, time, field_name, field_value from devinfo where field_name like '%_dot1x_radius_secret'"
- Should contain IP Address

Verify the Secret decrypts to the value entered for the devices on the EM and appliances.

- See Description for steps to allow decrypt of Secret.
- Enter: **fstool enc -d <hash value from DB>
- Enter: **fstool enc -d <hash value from fs_clients_enc.conf>

- Enter a Radius secret.

column2: DOT | Verify special characters can be used for the Radius Secret (wireless)
column6:
Special characters should be allowed for Radius secrets.
Note: If a Cisco switch and it will not take the character. Ex: ?
- Press 'Ctrl' + 'v'
- Then enter character Ex: ?

Also, on a 9.1.x system, in order to check encrypted value radius secret, you will need to modify this file:
/usr/local/forescout/lib/fstool/commands/enc.pl
and add the following lines and uncomment out 1 line:
add:
>my $clear = fs_decrypt($opt_d);
>print defined $clear ? "$clear\n" : "Not an encrypted string\n";

comment out:
>\#   die "Error: The '-d' flag is obsolete\n";

After editing, it should look like this:
![](index.php?/attachments/get/78938)
column9:

Note: There is a ' ' space between underscore and Plus characters.
